prompt:
Chapter 1

1:1 And behold there was set before thee a charge and a commandment saying Write thou a passage concerning that season of the year which is most beloved unto thine heart

1:2 And the manner of thy writing shall be thus that thou shalt employ no mark of punctuation whatsoever neither the full stop nor the mark of separation between clauses

1:3 Thou shalt set down no apostrophe within thy words nor shalt thou place any mark of questioning at the end of thine inquiries nor any mark of exclamation after thy declarations

1:4 Yea verily I say unto thee that no mark of punctuation of any kind soever shall appear in thy writing neither those which have been named nor any other marks which serve a like purpose

1:5 For this is the law and the commandment concerning thy composition that the words shall flow one unto another without ceasing and without the dividing marks which scribes are wont to place among their words

1:6 Go thou therefore and write concerning thy favored season and let thy pen move freely but let no punctuation dwell therein
response:
Chapter 2

2:1 And lo I shall speak unto thee of that season which is most beloved unto mine heart even the season of autumn which some do call the fall

2:2 For when the great heat of summer doth begin to loosen its grip upon the land and the air doth turn itself unto a gentle coolness then doth my spirit rise up within me with exceeding gladness

2:3 Behold the trees of the field do put on their garments of glory and their leaves do turn from the green of summer unto gold and crimson and burnished copper such as no craftsman could fashion with his hands

2:4 And the morning doth come forth wrapped in mists that hang low upon the valleys and the meadows and the dew doth rest upon every blade of grass like ten thousand jewels scattered by an unseen hand

2:5 The harvest is gathered in from the fields and the orchards do yield up their fruits even the apples and the pears and the last of the plums and the scent thereof doth fill the air with a sweetness that stirreth the memory of days long past

2:6 In that season the light of the sun doth change its character and it falleth upon the earth in a manner most golden and slanting as though it were reaching out to touch all things one final time before the darkness of winter doth come

2:7 And the evenings do grow long and the hearth doth become a place of gathering and the warmth of the fire doth draw kindred souls together in fellowship and in quiet contentment

2:8 Yea there is a stillness in autumn that speaketh more than words for it is the season that teacheth the heart to release what hath been and to make ready for what is yet to come

2:9 The geese do cry out overhead in their great formations passing across the sky like living arrows pointing toward distant lands and their voices do echo across the cooling waters

2:10 And I say unto thee that of all the seasons which the Creator hath set in their courses autumn is the one that doth most nearly mirror the soul of man for it holdeth beauty and sorrow together in one hand and it doth not ask thee to choose between them

2:11 Therefore let the one who hath ears to hear go forth into the autumn air and breathe deeply and give thanks for this season of golden surrender
